mappings.rationaleAI: X-ray crystallography reveals the internal atomic arrangements and crystal structures of minerals, directly addressing mineral structure and morphology (A.1), physical properties including optical characteristics (A.2), and the silicon-oxygen tetrahedron basis of silicate structure (A.3.b.i).

Symmetry principles formalized through group theory underpin crystallography and the classification of crystal systems, directly relevant to the internal structure and morphology of minerals (A.1) and their classification by crystal structure (A.3).

The periodic table's organization of 108 elements by chemical properties, from Big Bang nucleosynthesis of hydrogen and helium onward, provides the chemical foundation for mineral composition (A.1), the classification of nonsilicate minerals by their constituent elements (A.3.a), and the occurrence of elements in the Earth (C).

Ice exhibits at least fifteen distinct crystalline forms under varying pressures, directly relevant to mineral crystal structure and morphology (A.1) and physical properties (A.2). Water covering two-thirds of Earth's surface addresses the hydrosphere's elemental composition (C.4).

Volcanic processes from magma generation in the mantle to surface eruptions directly address the formation of igneous rocks from magma (B.1.c), extrusive rocks like basalt formed from lava flows (B.1.e.ii), and pyroclastic rocks like pumice and tuff from explosive eruptions (B.1.e.iii).

Britain's 600-million-year geological record encompasses Precambrian metamorphic rocks of the Scottish Highlands (B.3), volcanic episodes and igneous formations (B.1), Carboniferous coal swamps (B.5.a), desert sandstones and Cretaceous chalk as sedimentary rocks (B.2), and diverse rock associations from oceanic to mountain environments (B.4).

The emergence of geology as a science from ancient speculation through mining insights to the founding of the Geological Society addresses the systematic study of rocks (B) and the use of stratigraphy and fossils for understanding distribution of sedimentary rocks through time (B.2.f).

Plate tectonic processes of subduction, seafloor spreading, and continental collision govern magma formation (B.1.c) and determine rock associations across crustal environments including oceanic regions with basaltic lavas (B.4.a), island arcs with andesite lavas (B.4.d), and mountain ranges with metamorphic rocks and granitic batholiths (B.4.e).

Glacial periods produce tillites and other heterogeneous clastic sedimentary rocks from ice-transported debris (B.2.e.i), and glacial-interglacial cycles shape sedimentary sequences in stable continental regions including conglomerates and distinctive glacial deposits (B.4.b).

The solid inner core of iron and nickel and the liquid outer core directly address the occurrence of elements in the core (C.1), while convective flow in the liquid outer core and core-mantle interactions address mantle composition (C.2).

Earth's formation from a solar nebula and subsequent differentiation into distinct layers addresses the distribution and concentration of elements across the core (C.1), mantle (C.2), and crust (C.3) as the planet cooled and heavy elements sank inward.

Seismic wave analysis revealing Earth's layered internal structure — crust, mantle, and core — provides the primary observational evidence for understanding where elements occur in the core (C.1), mantle (C.2), and crust (C.3).
Asteroids as chemically unchanged primordial building blocks of the solar system provide direct evidence of the mineral and elemental composition found in meteorites and planetary bodies (A.4.b.iii).

Comets as frozen time capsules of ice and dust from the solar system's formation contain primordial mineral and chemical materials relevant to understanding mineral occurrence in meteorites and planetary bodies (A.4.b.iii).
